text: The surgeon opens the sigmoid mesocolon on the right side, extending the incision to the horizontal part of the duodenum. The retrorectal space is accessed, with careful separation along the rectal proper fascia to avoid the inferior hypogastric plexus within the subperitoneal fascia. After completing this separation, the incision in the sigmoid mesocolon is continued, exposing and ligating the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The dissection extends laterally, revealing the superior hypogastric plexus on the dorsal side, and the testicular blood vessels and ureter are dissected from the surrounding tissue.

Gauze is placed into the separated gap to guide the dissection route on the lateral peritoneum. The surgeon dissects the lateral peritoneum along the trijunction, freeing the sigmoid and descending colon. An incision is made above the peritoneal reflection, entering the anterior rectal space, with attention to the pelvic plexus while traversing the rectal collateral ligament.

The surgeon continues to separate along the rectal proper fascia until reaching the end of the Denon-Villiers fascia. At this level, the rectal proper fascia is absent. The muscle rectum is then opened, and under laparoscopic guidance, the rectal and pelvic muscles are further separated. The puborectalis muscle is visibly attached to the rectum, which is then carefully divided.

The surgeon continues to bear the rectum, entering the anal sphincter space at the edge of the levator ani muscle. Gauze is used to guide the transanal approach. After anal dilatation, an anus retractor provides a clear view of the tumor located on the rectal posterior wall, which has not invaded the anterior wall. A wedge resection is planned using a semi pouch suture to preserve the rectal mucous membrane receptor.

The surgeon excises the mucosa and enters the anal sphincter gap. The colon is pulled out through the anus, and the section is cut five centimeters away from the tumor. The colon and rectum are sewn together in stage one using a circular stapler. The circular suture at the distal intestine is loosened before knotting. The integrity of the margin is checked and sent for frozen pathology. The anastomosis is reinforced with interrupted sutures to prevent leakage.
The surgeon irrigates the abdominal cavity thoroughly and places a drainage tube in the pelvic cavity under laparoscopic guidance.
